headlines.title: Why did stock market crash today? Sensex ends over 700 points down, Nifty below 24,000

ripple_effect.name: Sudden stock market crash

historical_precedents.what_happened: US stock markets fell more than 20% in a single trading day — the largest one-day percentage decline in Dow Jones history — with the crash spreading to markets in Europe and Asia within hours.
historical_precedents.market_impact: The Dow Jones Industrial Average fell about 22.6% in one session; global markets lost a comparable share of their value over the following days.

historical_precedents.what_happened: US stock indices plunged roughly 9% in minutes before recovering most of the loss within the same session, driven in part by high-frequency algorithmic trading; regulators later introduced new circuit-breaker rules.
historical_precedents.market_impact: The Dow Jones dropped about 1,000 points intraday before rebounding; the episode led to new exchange-wide trading-halt rules.

historical_precedents.what_happened: Global stock markets fell roughly a third from their peak over about a month as the pandemic spread and lockdowns began, triggering multiple single-day trading halts.
historical_precedents.market_impact: The S&P 500 fell about 34% peak-to-trough; US markets hit circuit-breaker halts on four separate days in March 2020.

historical_precedents.what_happened: China's newly introduced circuit-breaker mechanism, only in effect for its fourth trading day, halted trading twice within minutes of the open after the CSI 300 index fell 7% — the second halt shut the market for the rest of the day in under 30 minutes; regulators suspended the mechanism entirely days later after concluding it had accelerated the sell-off rather than calmed it.
historical_precedents.market_impact: The Shanghai Composite fell roughly 7% in a single session on two separate days that week; the panic spread to European and US markets, contributing to one of the worst opening weeks for global stocks on record.

possibility_chains: A sudden, deep single-day stock decline has historically prompted exchanges to briefly halt trading via circuit breakers, giving markets time to reassess before reopening.
Sharp equity sell-offs have historically pushed investors toward traditionally safer assets such as government bonds and gold, temporarily lowering their yields and raising their prices.
Central banks have historically responded to severe market routs with public reassurances, emergency rate moves, or liquidity support when the decline threatened broader financial stability.
Extended market downturns have historically led companies to delay planned IPOs, financing rounds, and mergers until conditions stabilized.

headlines.title: Congo-Kinshasa: Ebola Outbreak Still Expanding, WHO Sees Signs of Stabilization

ripple_effect.name: Disease outbreak with international health emergency

historical_precedents.what_happened: The World Health Organization declared a Public Health Emergency of International Concern as Ebola spread across Guinea, Liberia, and Sierra Leone; several airlines suspended routes to the region and some countries imposed travel restrictions.
historical_precedents.market_impact: The World Bank estimated the outbreak cost the three hardest-hit countries over $2 billion in lost economic output.

historical_precedents.what_happened: The WHO declared a Public Health Emergency as Zika spread across the Americas, linked to birth defects; several countries issued travel advisories affecting tourism, notably ahead of the Rio de Janeiro Olympics.
historical_precedents.market_impact: Tourism-dependent economies in affected regions reported measurable declines in visitor bookings during the advisory period.

possibility_chains: A WHO-declared public health emergency has historically triggered travel advisories and, in some cases, temporary route suspensions by airlines.
Disease outbreaks with international travel warnings have historically reduced tourism and business travel to the affected region for the duration of the advisory.
Outbreaks affecting agricultural or manufacturing regions have historically disrupted local labor supply and production.
Vaccine and treatment developers have historically seen increased investment and expedited regulatory review during major outbreaks.
